perturbation.original_text: No later than [***] ([***]) Business Days following the end of each month during the Term, Company shall report to Janssen the number of Details performed (and any other information necessary to determine whether the requirements set forth in Section 3.2.2 and Exhibit B have been satisfied) during such month by the Sales Representatives in accordance with this Agreement and the Call Plan, as reported by the Company Internal Detail Reporting System.
perturbation.changed_text: No later than thirty (30) Business Days following the end of each quarter during the Term, Company shall report to Janssen the number of Details performed (and any other information necessary to determine whether the requirements set forth in Section 3.2.2 and Exhibit B have been satisfied) during such month by the Sales Representatives in accordance with this Agreement and the Call Plan, as reported by the Company Internal Detail Reporting System.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the reporting period from monthly to quarterly reduces the frequency of reporting and can potentially hide issues with sales practices violating the False Claims Act.
perturbation.law_explanation: The False Claims Act prohibits knowingly submitting false claims to the government. Delaying reporting on detailing activities could allow problematic sales practices (e.g., off-label promotion) to continue for a longer period, making it more difficult to detect and prevent violations, increasing the risk of false claims being submitted to government healthcare programs. Reducing the monitoring frequency can make it harder to meet the obligations imposed by the statute. Promotion of the drug must be fair and accurately portray the approved use of the medication.  Quarterly reporting could allow for the promotion to be stretched in an unlawful manner, due to the infrequency of checks and balances.

False claims (a) Liability for Certain Acts.- (1) In general .-Subject to paragraph (2), any person who- (A) knowingly presents, or causes to be presented, a false or fraudulent claim for payment or approval; (B) knowingly makes, uses, or causes to be made or used, a false record or statement material to a false or fraudulent claim; (C) conspires to commit a violation of
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Civil Division | The False Claims Act Skip to main content MENU Fraud Section Practice Areas The False Claims Act Press Releases Fraud Recovery Statistics (PDF) Report Fraud Contact the Fraud Section The False Claims Act Share Facebook X LinkedIn Email Many of the Fraud Section’s cases are suits filed under the False Claims Act (FCA), 31 U.S.C. §§ 3729 - 3733, a federal statute originally enacted in 1863 in response to defense contractor fraud during the American Civil War. The FCA provides that any person who knowingly submits, or causes to submit, false claims to the government is liable for three times the government’s damages plus a penalty that is linked to inflation . FCA liability can arise in other situations, such as when someone knowingly uses a false record material to a false claim or improperly avoids an obligation to pay the government. Conspiring to commit any of these acts also is a violation of the FCA. In addition to allowing the United States to pursue perpetrators of fraud on its own, the FCA allows private citizens to file suits on behalf of the government (called “ qui tam ” suits) against those who have defrauded the government. Private citizens who successfully bring qui tam actions may receive a portion of the government’s recovery. Many Fraud Section investigations and lawsuits arise from such qui tam actions. The Department of Justice obtained more than $2.9 billion in settlements and judgments from civil cases involving fraud and false claims against the government in the fiscal year ending Sept. 30, 2024. More information about those recoveries can be found here and the 2024 FCA statistics can be found here .” Updated January 15, 2025
perturbation.justification: The change from monthly to quarterly reporting creates a legal contradiction. The False Claims Act, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 2, addresses false claims submitted to the government. The Law Explanation states delaying reporting on detailing activities allows potentially problematic sales practices to continue longer, which increases the risk of false claims. Reducing the frequency of monitoring makes it harder to meet obligations imposed by the statute. The original contract has a higher standard for transparency which has been reduced in the changed text. This violates the principles of the False Claims Act, because a company promoting a product could be acting unlawfully during the longer lapse in time and be in violation of the FCA, 31 U.S.C. §§ 3729 - 3733, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 2.

perturbation.original_text: Except to the extent expressly authorized by this Agreement or otherwise agreed in writing, each of Janssen and Company agrees that, during the Term and until the conclusion of the [***] ([***]) year period beginning upon the expiration or earlier termination of this Agreement, such Party shall: (a) maintain in confidence the Confidential Information of the other Party using not less than the efforts such Party uses to maintain in confidence its own confidential or proprietary information of similar kind and value (but not less than reasonable efforts); (b) not disclose the Confidential Information of the other Party to any Third Party; and (c) not use the Confidential Information of the other Party for any purpose other than as provided for in this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Except to the extent expressly authorized by this Agreement or otherwise agreed in writing, each of Janssen and Company agrees that, during the Term and until the conclusion of the one (1) year period beginning upon the expiration or earlier termination of this Agreement, such Party shall: (a) maintain in confidence the Confidential Information of the other Party using not less than the efforts such Party uses to maintain in confidence its own confidential or proprietary information of similar kind and value (but not less than reasonable efforts); (b) not disclose the Confidential Information of the other Party to any Third Party; and (c) not use the Confidential Information of the other Party for any purpose other than as provided for in this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year for trade secrets is insufficient to protect confidential information. This exposes the other party to losing trade secret protection if the confidential information is still valuable or in use beyond that period. While the contract is governed by New York law, the trade secrets could be leveraged anywhere, so the UTSA and the Defend Trade Secrets Act are applicable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings to protect trade secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) aims to protect trade secrets from misappropriation.  Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be considered commercially unreasonable, especially for trade secrets with a longer lifespan or development cycle. This shorter period undermines the protection afforded by the DTSA, which doesn't prescribe a specific duration, but implies reasonable measures to maintain secrecy. If an actual trade secret is still valuable after a year, this would be inadequate protection of a trade secret under the DTSA. While the UTSA itself is implemented on the state level, it provides a cause of action for misappropriation, which should be defended against, but is undermined by a short confidentiality window.

(2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period for trade secrets to one year, potentially contradicting the protections offered by trade secret law. The Perturbation and Law explanations argue that this timeframe might be insufficient to safeguard valuable trade secrets, especially those with longer lifecycles. Snippet 1, referencing 18 USC 1836, indicates that owners of misappropriated trade secrets can bring civil actions. The reduced confidentiality period weakens the ability to protect against misappropriation and, therefore, creates a legal contradiction, making 'YES' the appropriate answer.

perturbation.original_text: Company shall, and shall cause each of its Sales Representatives and Other Company Employees to, notify Janssen as soon as possible, but no later than 24 hours after the time he or she becomes aware of any Product Complaint associated with the Product, which notice shall include the name of the person making such Product Complaint, the Target that prescribed the Product (if any), and the date the relevant Sales Representative or Other Company Employee received such Product Complaint.
perturbation.changed_text: Company shall, and shall cause each of its Sales Representatives and Other Company Employees to, notify Janssen no later than 72 hours after the time he or she becomes aware of any Product Complaint associated with the Product, which notice shall include the name of the person making such Product Complaint, the Target that prescribed the Product (if any), and the date the relevant Sales Representative or Other Company Employee received such Product Complaint.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the reporting time for product complaints from 24 to 72 hours is non-compliant with FDA regulations concerning timely reporting of adverse events. It would take longer to recall a defective drug, potentially injuring consumers.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 21 CFR 314.80 - Postmarketing reporting of adverse drug experiences
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR 314.80 outlines requirements for postmarketing reporting of adverse drug experiences. While this section covers adverse drug experiences and not all product complaints, delaying the notification of product complaints could delay the identification of potentially serious issues or adverse events, leading to a violation of the regulation. The FDA emphasizes the importance of prompt reporting to ensure timely action regarding product safety. A 72-hour delay may impede timely investigation and reporting of issues.

perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the notification timeframe for product complaints from 24 to 72 hours, which creates an inconsistency with timely reporting expectations. The Law Explanation states that delaying notification of product complaints "could delay the identification of potentially serious issues or adverse events, leading to a violation of the regulation." While the provided law snippet (21 CFR 314.80) specifically discusses 'postmarketing reporting of adverse drug experiences' and not all product complaints, the Law Explanation reasonably connects the delay to potentially delaying the identification of serious issues, impacting timely action and creating a violation. The Perturbation Explanation emphasizes this point by noting that delaying reporting could delay recalling a defective drug, potentially injuring consumers. Therefore, the modification introduces a significant inconsistency regarding the timeframe for reporting potentially harmful product issues.
